div.h2: ROTR vs SHR: Bit-Level Comparison (32-bit word)
div.p: σ functions combine BOTH operations for full bit dispersion

g.text: KEY INSIGHT: Why BOTH Operations Are Required
Design rationale for ROTR + SHR combination in σ functions
ROTR alone | Bijective | Schedule would be reversible → attacker can invert W→message
SHR alone | Lossy | Too much information destroyed → poor diffusion
ROTR+SHR | Optimal | Full diffusion (ROTR) + irreversibility (SHR) achieved
C macro | SHR32 | ((uint32_t)(x) >> n) — trailing bits permanently lost
Warning | uint32_t | MUST use unsigned — signed left-shift is UB in C
bit 0 | 0 | ← was bit 3 of x LSB
PRESERVED | 32/32 bits | ALL bits survive rotation
bit 0 | 0 | ← was bit 3 of x LSB
DESTROYED | 3/32 bits | bits 0,1,2 of x are GONE forever
σ0 used in message schedule for W[t-15]
ROTR(x,7) | view 1 | rotated perspective — ALL 32 bits present
ROTR(x,18) | view 2 | rotated perspective — ALL 32 bits present
SHR(x,3) | view 3 | truncated perspective — bits 0,1,2 zeroed
XOR result | output | each output bit depends on 3 different input positions
IRREVERSIBLE | design goal | SHR prevents schedule inversion
σ1 used in message schedule for W[t-2]
ROTR(x,17) | view 1 | rotated perspective — ALL 32 bits present
ROTR(x,19) | view 2 | rotated perspective — ALL 32 bits present
SHR(x,10) | view 3 | truncated perspective — bits 0-9 zeroed
XOR result | output | wide asymmetry vs σ0 prevents structural symmetry
IRREVERSIBLE | design goal | SHR prevents schedule inversion
